verses.explainer: As it is written: 'Whoever gathered much had nothing left over, and whoever gathered little had no lack.' The manna principle: God provides enough for everyone when the community shares.

verses.text: But thanks be to God, who puts the same earnest care for you into the heart of Titus.
verses.explainer: Thanks be to God who put the same earnest care for the Corinthians into the heart of Titus. Titus' concern for them is itself a gift from God.

verses.text: For he indeed accepted our exhortation, but being himself very earnest, he went out to you of his own accord.
verses.explainer: Titus accepted Paul's appeal but is also coming to Corinth of his own accord. He is not a reluctant messenger but an eager partner.

verses.text: We have sent together with him the brother whose praise in the Good News is known throughout all the assemblies.
verses.explainer: Paul is sending along with Titus a brother who is famous among all the churches for his preaching of the gospel. This unnamed brother's identity has been debated for centuries -- Luke, Barnabas, and Apollos are the most common guesses.

verses.text: Not only so, but he was also appointed by the assemblies to travel with us in this grace, which is served by us to the glory of the Lord himself, and to show our readiness.
verses.explainer: This brother was appointed by the churches to travel with Paul in administering the collection. Having church-appointed companions ensures financial transparency and accountability.

verses.text: We are avoiding this, that any man should blame us concerning this abundance which is administered by us.
verses.explainer: Paul takes this precaution so that no one can blame him for how the generous gift is administered. Financial integrity in ministry is not optional -- Paul goes out of his way to ensure above-reproach handling of money.

thenVsNow.then: Ancient religious leaders were frequently accused of financial corruption. The temples of pagan gods often served as banks, and priests controlled enormous wealth.
thenVsNow.now: Financial scandals continue to damage religious institutions. Paul's insistence on accountability, third-party oversight, and transparency remains the gold standard for handling donated funds.

verses.text: Having regard for honorable things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men.
verses.explainer: Paul aims for what is honorable not only in the Lord's sight but also in the sight of man. Internal integrity is not enough -- there must also be the appearance of integrity. Paul anticipates the modern principle of avoiding even the appearance of impropriety.

verses.text: We have sent with them our brother, whom we have many times proved earnest in many things, but now much more earnest, by reason of the great confidence which he has in you.
verses.explainer: Paul sends yet another brother whom he has often tested and found earnest. Three delegates are coming -- Titus and two unnamed brothers -- ensuring a team approach to financial oversight.

verses.text: As for Titus, he is my partner and fellow worker for you. As for our brothers, they are the apostles of the assemblies, the glory of Christ.
verses.explainer: Titus is Paul's partner and fellow worker for the Corinthians. The other brothers are messengers of the churches, the glory of Christ. Paul gives all three the highest commendation.

verses.text: Therefore show the proof of your love to them before the assemblies, and of our boasting on your behalf.
verses.explainer: Paul asks the Corinthians to show these men the proof of their love and to vindicate Paul's boasting about them to all the churches. The Corinthians' generosity will either confirm or embarrass Paul's confidence in them.

explainer.summary: Paul continues his appeal for the collection, explaining that he has been boasting to the Macedonians about the Corinthians' readiness. He urges them to have their gift ready so neither he nor they will be embarrassed. The chapter contains some of the Bible's best-known principles of giving: God loves a cheerful giver, whoever sows generously will also reap generously, and God is able to make all grace abound so that you always have enough for every good work.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter completes the collection appeal. It moves from practical logistics to theological vision: generosity is not just meeting a need but producing a cascade of thanksgiving to God. The collection is worship in financial form.

wordStudies.meaning: Joyful, glad, willing. The English word 'hilarious' derives from it. God does not want grudging compliance but joyful generosity that delights in giving.

verses.text: It is indeed unnecessary for me to write to you concerning the service to the saints,
verses.explainer: It is superfluous for Paul to write about the ministry to the saints. He knows their readiness. This polite opening is also a gentle prod -- if they are truly ready, they should prove it.

verses.text: for I know your readiness, of which I boast on your behalf to them of Macedonia, that Achaia has been prepared for a year past. Your zeal has stirred up very many of them.
verses.explainer: Paul has been boasting about the Corinthians' eagerness to the Macedonians, saying that Achaia has been ready since last year. Their zeal stirred up most of the Macedonians. Now Paul needs the Corinthians to deliver on the promise he made on their behalf.

verses.text: But I have sent the brothers that our boasting on your behalf may not be in vain in this respect, that, just as I said, you may be prepared,
verses.explainer: Paul is sending the brothers ahead so that his boasting about the Corinthians will not prove empty. He wants the gift to be ready when he arrives, not assembled under last-minute pressure.

verses.text: lest by any means, if anyone from Macedonia comes there with me and finds you unprepared, we (to say nothing of you) would be disappointed in this confident boasting.
verses.explainer: If Macedonians come with Paul and find the Corinthians unprepared, the humiliation would fall on Paul and the Corinthians alike. This is practical leadership -- Paul is transparent about the stakes.

verses.text: I thought it necessary therefore to entreat the brothers that they would go before to you, and arrange ahead of time the generous gift that you promised before, that the same might be ready as a matter of generosity, and not of greediness.
verses.explainer: Paul wants the gift to be ready as a willing offering, not as an exaction. The word for 'exaction' (pleonexia) can also mean 'greediness.' The gift should reflect generosity, not reluctant obligation.

verses.text: Remember this: he who sows sparingly will also reap sparingly. He who sows bountifully will also reap bountifully.
verses.explainer: Whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully. The agricultural metaphor is straightforward: you harvest in proportion to what you plant. Generosity produces abundance.

verses.text: Let each man give according as he has determined in his heart; not grudgingly, or under compulsion; for God loves a cheerful giver.
verses.explainer: Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver. This is one of the most quoted verses on giving. Three principles: individual decision, freedom from pressure, and joyful willingness.

thenVsNow.then: Temple taxes and tithes in Judaism were obligatory. Paul's insistence on voluntary, cheerful giving represented a significant shift in how religious communities funded their work.
thenVsNow.now: Churches still debate whether tithing (10% of income) is mandatory for Christians or whether Paul's principle of cheerful, voluntary generosity replaced the fixed percentage. This verse is central to that debate.

verses.text: And God is able to make all grace abound to you, that you, always having all sufficiency in everything, may abound to every good work.
verses.explainer: God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work. Paul piles up 'all' language to emphasize God's unlimited provision. The purpose of abundance is not accumulation but generosity.

verses.text:
As it is written,
“He has scattered abroad, he has given to the poor.
His righteousness remains forever.”
verses.explainer: As it is written: 'He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever.' Paul quotes Psalm 112:9, describing the righteous person whose generosity endures eternally.

verses.text: Now may he who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food, supply and multiply your seed for sowing, and increase the fruits of your righteousness;
verses.explainer: God who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food will supply and multiply the Corinthians' seed for sowing and increase the harvest of their righteousness. God provides both the resources to give and the results of giving.

verses.text: you being enriched in everything to all generosity, which produces through us thanksgiving to God.
verses.explainer: They will be enriched in every way to be generous in every way, which through Paul produces thanksgiving to God. Enrichment is for the purpose of generosity, and generosity produces worship. The cycle is: God gives, believers give, recipients give thanks to God.

verses.text: For this service of giving that you perform not only makes up for lack among the saints, but abounds also through much giving of thanks to God;
verses.explainer: This ministry not only supplies the needs of the saints but also overflows in many thanksgivings to God. The collection has a double effect: meeting physical needs and generating spiritual worship.

verses.text: seeing that through the proof given by this service, they glorify God for the obedience of your confession to the Good News of Christ, and for the generosity of your contribution to them and to all;
verses.explainer: By their proven character through this ministry, the Jerusalem believers will glorify God for the Corinthians' submission to the gospel of Christ and for their generosity in sharing. The collection is a confession of faith -- it proves that the gospel has transformed the Corinthians' relationship with money.

verses.text: while they themselves also, with supplication on your behalf, yearn for you by reason of the exceeding grace of God in you.
verses.explainer: The Jerusalem believers will pray for the Corinthians with deep affection because of the surpassing grace of God upon them. The gift creates a relational bond between communities that have never met face to face.

verses.text: Now thanks be to God for his unspeakable gift!
verses.explainer: Thanks be to God for his indescribable gift! Paul closes the collection appeal by pointing to the ultimate gift -- God's gift of Christ, which is the source and model for all Christian generosity.

explainer.summary: The tone shifts dramatically. Paul addresses those who accuse him of being 'bold in letters but timid in person.' He insists that his weapons are not worldly but divinely powerful, capable of destroying strongholds and every argument raised against God. He warns his opponents that when he comes, his actions will match his letters. The contrast between Paul's self-effacing style and his opponents' self-promotion could not be sharper.
themes: Paul's authority in person and in letter
explainer.contextInBook: Chapters 10-13 form a distinct unit that many scholars believe was originally a separate letter (possibly the 'severe letter' Paul mentions). The tone shifts from the warm reconciliation of chapters 1-9 to fierce polemic. Paul directly engages the 'super-apostles' who are undermining him.

wordStudies.meaning: A military fortification. Paul uses it metaphorically for deeply entrenched patterns of thought that resist God's truth. His spiritual weapons are designed to demolish these intellectual and spiritual defenses.

verses.text: Now I Paul, myself, entreat you by the humility and gentleness of Christ; I who in your presence am lowly among you, but being absent am bold toward you.
verses.explainer: Paul appeals by the meekness and gentleness of Christ -- this same Paul who is accused of being humble when face to face but bold at a distance. He turns the accusation into a Christlike virtue.

thenVsNow.then: In Greco-Roman rhetoric, a speaker's physical presence and forcefulness were considered essential to authority. Paul's meek demeanor in person was seen as weakness.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between gentle presence and strong written communication resonates in the age of email and social media, where people often seem bolder online than in person.

verses.text: Yes, I beg you that I may not, when present, show courage with the confidence with which I intend to be bold against some, who consider us to be walking according to the flesh.
verses.explainer: Paul begs not to have to show boldness when he comes, against those who think he walks 'according to the flesh.' His opponents accuse him of operating by worldly standards.

verses.text: For though we walk in the flesh, we don’t wage war according to the flesh;
verses.explainer: Though Paul walks in the flesh, he does not wage war according to the flesh. He lives in a human body but fights with supernatural weapons.

verses.text: for the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh, but mighty before God to the throwing down of strongholds,
verses.explainer: The weapons of Paul's warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds. This is the foundational text for understanding spiritual warfare in Paul's thought -- it is primarily intellectual and argumentative, not mystical.

verses.text: throwing down imaginations and every high thing that is exalted against the knowledge of God, and bringing every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ;
verses.explainer: Paul destroys arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and takes every thought captive to obey Christ. Spiritual warfare is waged in the realm of ideas. The battlefield is the mind.

verses.text: and being in readiness to avenge all disobedience, when your obedience will be made full.
verses.explainer: Paul is ready to punish every disobedience when the Corinthians' obedience is complete. He will deal with the rebels, but first he wants the loyal majority to demonstrate their allegiance clearly.

verses.text: Do you look at things only as they appear in front of your face? If anyone trusts in himself that he is Christ’s, let him consider this again with himself, that, even as he is Christ’s, so also we are Christ’s.
verses.explainer: Paul challenges them to look at what is right in front of them. If anyone is confident that they belong to Christ, they should consider that Paul belongs to Christ just as much as they do.

verses.text: For though I should boast somewhat abundantly concerning our authority, (which the Lord gave for building you up, and not for casting you down) I will not be disappointed,
verses.explainer: Even if Paul boasts somewhat freely about his authority -- which the Lord gave for building up, not tearing down -- he will not be ashamed. His authority serves a constructive purpose.

verses.text: that I may not seem as if I desire to terrify you by my letters.
verses.explainer: Paul does not want to seem to be frightening them with his letters. He anticipates and preempts the accusation that he is all bark and no bite.

verses.text: For, “His letters”, they say, “are weighty and strong, but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech is despised.”
verses.explainer: His opponents say: 'His letters are weighty and strong, but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech of no account.' This is a direct quote from Paul's critics. They find his letters impressive but his person unimpressive.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Physical presence and rhetoric', 'explanation': "In Greco-Roman culture, a speaker's body, voice, and delivery were considered as important as the content of their message. Paul apparently lacked the commanding physical presence and polished oratory his opponents possessed."}

verses.text: Let such a person consider this, that what we are in word by letters when we are absent, such are we also in deed when we are present.
verses.explainer: Let such people understand that what Paul says by letter when absent, he will do when present. This is a warning: do not mistake Paul's gentleness for weakness.

verses.text: For we are not bold to number or compare ourselves with some of those who commend themselves. But they themselves, measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves with themselves, are without understanding.
verses.explainer: Paul will not dare to compare himself with those who commend themselves. When they measure themselves by one another and compare themselves with one another, they are without understanding. Self-referential evaluation is meaningless.

verses.text: But we will not boast beyond proper limits, but within the boundaries with which God appointed to us, which reach even to you.
verses.explainer: Paul will not boast beyond limits but will confine his boasting to the area God assigned to him, which includes Corinth. Paul claims divine authorization for his territory of ministry.

verses.text: For we don’t stretch ourselves too much, as though we didn’t reach to you. For we came even as far as to you with the Good News of Christ,
verses.explainer: Paul is not overextending himself -- he was the first to come to Corinth with the gospel of Christ. His opponents arrived after him. He has prior claim to this church.

verses.text: not boasting beyond proper limits in other men’s labors, but having hope that as your faith grows, we will be abundantly enlarged by you in our sphere of influence,
verses.explainer: Paul does not boast in other people's work. His hope is that as the Corinthians' faith increases, his area of influence will be greatly enlarged through them.

verses.text: so as to preach the Good News even to the parts beyond you, not to boast in what someone else has already done.
verses.explainer: Paul wants to preach the gospel in lands beyond Corinth, without boasting about work already done in someone else's area. He respects missionary boundaries and wants to break new ground.

verses.text: But “he who boasts, let him boast in the Lord.”
verses.explainer: Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord. Paul quotes Jeremiah 9:24 as the final standard: the only legitimate boast is in what God has done.

verses.text: For it isn’t he who commends himself who is approved, but whom the Lord commends.
verses.explainer: It is not the one who commends himself who is approved, but the one whom the Lord commends. Self-promotion means nothing. Only God's evaluation counts.

explainer.summary: Paul engages in what he calls 'foolish' boasting -- matching his opponents' self-promotion while subverting it at every turn. He is jealous for the Corinthians with a godly jealousy, fearing they are being seduced by a different Jesus. He provides the most detailed catalog of his sufferings anywhere in the New Testament: beatings, shipwrecks, imprisonments, hunger, and constant danger. His 'boasting' is in his weaknesses, not his achievements.
themes: Foolish boasting that subverts worldly values
Weakness as the true credential of ministry
explainer.contextInBook: This is Paul at his most rhetorically brilliant and emotionally intense. The 'fool's speech' (11:1-12:13) is a masterpiece of ironic self-defense. Paul enters the boasting contest his opponents started, but he boasts about the very things they would consider disqualifying.

wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'super-duper apostles' -- Paul's sarcastic label for the rival teachers who claimed superiority over him. The prefix hyper- (beyond, above) is used mockingly.
Without sense, foolish. Paul repeatedly calls himself a fool for engaging in self-promotion, because he knows boasting is inherently foolish. But his opponents have forced his hand.

verses.text: I wish that you would bear with me in a little foolishness, but indeed you do bear with me.
verses.explainer: Paul asks the Corinthians to bear with a little foolishness from him. He is about to engage in self-promotion, which he considers absurd, but his opponents have left him no choice.

verses.text: For I am jealous over you with a godly jealousy. For I married you to one husband, that I might present you as a pure virgin to Christ.
verses.explainer: Paul is jealous for the Corinthians with a divine jealousy. He betrothed them to one husband, Christ, and wants to present them as a pure virgin. The metaphor casts Paul as the father of the bride arranging the marriage.

verses.text: But I am afraid that somehow, as the serpent deceived Eve in his craftiness, so your minds might be corrupted from the simplicity that is in Christ.
verses.explainer: Paul fears that as the serpent deceived Eve by his cunning, the Corinthians' thoughts will be led astray from sincere and pure devotion to Christ. The super-apostles are cast as the serpent in the garden.

verses.text: For if he who comes preaches another Jesus, whom we did not preach, or if you receive a different spirit, which you did not receive, or a different “good news”, which you did not accept, you put up with that well enough.
verses.explainer: If someone comes and proclaims another Jesus, a different spirit, or a different gospel, the Corinthians put up with it readily enough. Paul is astonished at their gullibility -- they tolerate false teaching more readily than they tolerate him.

verses.text: For I reckon that I am not at all behind the very best apostles.
verses.explainer: Paul considers himself not at all inferior to these 'super-apostles.' The sarcasm drips. He will now prove his point -- but not with the credentials they value.

verses.text: But though I am unskilled in speech, yet I am not unskilled in knowledge. No, in every way we have been revealed to you in all things.
verses.explainer: Even if Paul is unskilled in speaking, he is not unskilled in knowledge. He has made this clear in every way. Paul concedes that his rhetorical delivery may be lacking but insists his message is sound.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Rhetorical skill', 'explanation': "Corinth was a city that prized eloquent speakers. Sophists (traveling orators) were celebrities. Paul's opponents apparently had superior speaking abilities. Paul admits this but argues that content matters more than style."}

verses.text: Or did I commit a sin in humbling myself that you might be exalted, because I preached to you God’s Good News free of charge?
verses.explainer: Did Paul commit a sin by humbling himself so that they might be exalted, by preaching God's gospel free of charge? This rhetorical question exposes the absurdity: his opponents charge fees and are respected; Paul works for free and is criticized.

thenVsNow.then: In Greco-Roman culture, refusing payment could signal low status -- only slaves worked for free. Professional teachers expected compensation, and higher fees implied higher quality.
thenVsNow.now: The debate about whether ministers should accept payment or work 'tent-making' jobs continues. Paul's example shows that context determines the right approach -- he accepted support from some churches but not from Corinth.

verses.text: I robbed other assemblies, taking wages from them that I might serve you.
verses.explainer: Paul 'robbed' other churches by accepting support from them to serve the Corinthians. The language is deliberately provocative. He took money from Macedonia to serve Corinth for free.

verses.text: When I was present with you and was in need, I wasn’t a burden on anyone, for the brothers, when they came from Macedonia, supplied the measure of my need. In everything I kept myself from being burdensome to you, and I will continue to do so.
verses.explainer: When Paul was in need in Corinth, he did not burden anyone. Brothers from Macedonia supplied what he needed. He kept himself from being a burden and will continue to do so.

verses.text: As the truth of Christ is in me, no one will stop me from this boasting in the regions of Achaia.
verses.explainer: As the truth of Christ is in Paul, this boast of his will not be silenced in the regions of Achaia. He is proud of his financial independence and will not let anyone take this boast from him.

verses.text: Why? Because I don’t love you? God knows.
verses.explainer: Why does Paul refuse Corinthian money? Because he does not love them? God knows he does. This question reveals the hurt: some Corinthians interpreted his refusal of payment as rejection.

verses.text: But what I do, that I will do, that I may cut off occasion from them that desire an occasion, that in which they boast, they may be found even as we.
verses.explainer: Paul will continue refusing payment to undercut those who want an opportunity to be regarded as equal to him. If Paul charged nothing and they charged fees, who is the genuine article?

verses.text: For such men are false apostles, deceitful workers, masquerading as Christ’s apostles.
verses.explainer: These people are false apostles, deceitful workers, disguising themselves as apostles of Christ. The gloves come off. Paul drops the sarcasm and makes a direct accusation.

verses.text: And no wonder, for even Satan masquerades as an angel of light.
verses.explainer: Even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light. Paul's ultimate charge: the super-apostles' attractive exterior hides satanic deception.

verses.text: It is no great thing therefore if his servants also masquerade as servants of righteousness, whose end will be according to their works.
verses.explainer: It is no surprise that Satan's servants disguise themselves as servants of righteousness. Their end will correspond to their deeds. Paul predicts divine judgment on the false teachers.

verses.text: I say again, let no one think me foolish. But if so, yet receive me as foolish, that I also may boast a little.
verses.explainer: Paul repeats: let no one think him foolish. But even if they do, let them accept him as a fool so he can do a little boasting. He is entering the boasting contest against his better judgment.

verses.text: That which I speak, I don’t speak according to the Lord, but as in foolishness, in this confidence of boasting.
verses.explainer: What Paul is about to say, he says not according to the Lord but as a fool in this business of boasting. He explicitly disclaims divine authority for the form (not the content) of what follows. Self-promotion is inherently foolish.

verses.text: Seeing that many boast after the flesh, I will also boast.
verses.explainer: Since many boast according to the flesh, Paul will boast too. He adopts the method to expose its absurdity.

verses.text: For you bear with the foolish gladly, being wise.
verses.explainer: The Corinthians gladly bear with fools, being wise themselves. The irony is cutting -- if they are so wise, why do they tolerate fools? And if they tolerate the super-apostles' foolish boasting, they can tolerate Paul's.

verses.text: For you bear with a man, if he brings you into bondage, if he devours you, if he takes you captive, if he exalts himself, if he strikes you on the face.
verses.explainer: The Corinthians put up with anyone who enslaves them, devours them, takes advantage of them, puts on airs, or strikes them in the face. Paul catalogs the abuses of the super-apostles in escalating severity.

verses.text: I speak by way of disparagement, as though we had been weak. Yet in whatever way anyone is bold (I speak in foolishness), I am bold also.
verses.explainer: Paul says with shame that he was 'too weak' for such behavior. Then he pivots: whatever anyone else dares to boast about, Paul dares too. The irony is that his 'weakness' is actually his integrity.

verses.text: Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they the offspring of Abraham? So am I.
verses.explainer: Are they Hebrews? So is Paul. Israelites? So is he. Offspring of Abraham? So is he. Paul can match their ethnic and religious credentials point for point.

verses.text: Are they servants of Christ? (I speak as one beside himself) I am more so; in labors more abundantly, in prisons more abundantly, in stripes above measure, in deaths often.
verses.explainer: Are they servants of Christ? Paul is a better one -- and he speaks as a fool in saying it. Then the flood gates open: far greater labors, far more imprisonments, countless beatings, often near death.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Paul's suffering catalog", 'explanation': "This is the most extensive autobiography Paul ever provides. Many of these events are not recorded in Acts, revealing that Luke's account covers only a fraction of Paul's experiences."}

verses.text: Five times from the Jews I received forty stripes minus one.
verses.explainer: Five times Paul received from the Jews the forty lashes minus one. The Jewish punishment was thirty-nine stripes -- one less than the lethal forty. Paul endured this five times, meaning he submitted to synagogue authority and kept returning to Jewish communities despite the beatings.

verses.text: Three times I was beaten with rods. Once I was stoned. Three times I suffered shipwreck. I have been a night and a day in the deep.
verses.explainer: Three times Paul was beaten with rods, once stoned, three times shipwrecked, and he spent a night and a day adrift at sea. Acts records one rod-beating (Philippi) and one stoning (Lystra), but Paul experienced far more than what Luke narrated.

verses.text: I have been in travels often, perils of rivers, perils of robbers, perils from my countrymen, perils from the Gentiles, perils in the city, perils in the wilderness, perils in the sea, perils among false brothers;
verses.explainer: On frequent journeys, in danger from rivers, robbers, his own people, Gentiles, cities, wilderness, the sea, and false brothers. Danger from every direction. The ancient world was genuinely dangerous for travelers, and Paul's controversial message made him a target everywhere.

verses.text: in labor and travail, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, and in cold and nakedness.
verses.explainer: In toil and hardship, through many a sleepless night, in hunger and thirst, often without food, in cold and exposure. The physical toll of Paul's ministry was extreme. This is not metaphorical -- he was frequently without adequate food, sleep, or shelter.

verses.text: Besides those things that are outside, there is that which presses on me daily: anxiety for all the assemblies.
verses.explainer: Apart from all these external pressures, there is the daily anxiety Paul feels for all the churches. Physical suffering is complemented by emotional burden. He carries the weight of every congregation in his mind.

verses.text: Who is weak, and I am not weak? Who is caused to stumble, and I don’t burn with indignation?
verses.explainer: Who is weak, and Paul is not weak? Who is made to stumble, and Paul is not indignant? He identifies so completely with each church member that their struggles become his own.

verses.text: If I must boast, I will boast of the things that concern my weakness.
verses.explainer: If Paul must boast, he will boast of the things that show his weakness. This is the key to the entire fool's speech: Paul enters the boasting contest and wins by losing. His credentials are his scars.

thenVsNow.then: Every item in Paul's suffering catalog would have been considered evidence of failure by Greco-Roman standards. A successful person would not have been beaten, shipwrecked, or hungry.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's inversion of success metrics challenges cultures that equate visible achievement with divine favor. His 'resume of failures' remains countercultural in any era that worships success.

verses.text: The God and Father of the Lord Jesus Christ, he who is blessed forever more, knows that I don’t lie.
verses.explainer: The God and Father of the Lord Jesus knows that Paul is not lying. He invokes God as witness to the truthfulness of his catalog. The sufferings are real, not exaggerated.

verses.text: In Damascus the governor under King Aretas guarded the Damascenes’ city, desiring to arrest me.
verses.explainer: In Damascus, the governor under King Aretas guarded the city in order to seize Paul. This is the earliest datable event in Paul's autobiography -- his escape from Damascus, which occurred shortly after his conversion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Aretas IV', 'explanation': "Aretas IV was the Nabataean king whose daughter Herod Antipas had divorced to marry Herodias. His control or influence over Damascus in this period helps date Paul's conversion to the early-to-mid 30s AD."}

verses.text: Through a window I was let down in a basket by the wall, and escaped his hands.
verses.explainer: Paul was let down in a basket through a window in the wall and escaped. He closes his suffering catalog with the most undignified image possible -- a grown man being lowered from a city wall in a basket. His opponents boasted of triumphs; Paul boasts of a humiliating escape.

explainer.summary: Paul describes a man (himself, speaking in the third person) who was caught up to the third heaven fourteen years ago and heard things too sacred to repeat. But lest he become conceited, he was given a 'thorn in the flesh' -- a chronic affliction he begged God three times to remove. God's answer: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.' This is the theological climax of the letter and one of the most profound passages in Scripture.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter brings the fool's speech to its climax. Paul's opponents boasted of spiritual experiences; Paul can match them (third heaven!) but immediately pivots to his weakness. The thorn in the flesh passage is the theological summit of 2 Corinthians -- everything in the letter has been building to 'my power is made perfect in weakness.'

wordStudies.translatedAs: A 'stake' or 'thorn' embedded in the body
wordStudies.meaning: A 'stake' or 'thorn' embedded in the body. The exact nature of Paul's affliction is unknown -- theories include chronic eye disease, malaria, epilepsy, migraines, speech impediment, and spiritual opposition. Paul deliberately does not specify, perhaps so that every suffering reader can identify with him.

wordStudies.translatedAs: In Jewish cosmology, multiple heavens existed
wordStudies.meaning: In Jewish cosmology, multiple heavens existed. The first was the atmosphere, the second the stars, and the third was God's throne room -- the highest heaven. Being 'caught up' there meant being in the immediate presence of God.

verses.text: It is doubtless not profitable for me to boast. For I will come to visions and revelations of the Lord.
verses.explainer: Paul must go on boasting, though there is nothing to be gained by it. He will move to visions and revelations of the Lord. His opponents claimed ecstatic spiritual experiences; Paul can match them.

verses.text: I know a man in Christ, fourteen years ago (whether in the body, I don’t know, or whether out of the body, I don’t know; God knows), such a one caught up into the third heaven.
verses.explainer: Paul knows a man in Christ who fourteen years ago was caught up to the third heaven -- whether in the body or out of the body, he does not know. The third-person narration is a deliberate distancing device. Paul describes the experience without claiming it as a personal achievement.

verses.text: I know such a man (whether in the body, or outside of the body, I don’t know; God knows),
verses.explainer: Paul repeats: whether in the body or out of the body, he does not know -- God knows. The repeated uncertainty about embodiment suggests the experience was so overwhelming that normal categories of physical awareness did not apply.

verses.text: how he was caught up into Paradise, and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter.
verses.explainer: He was caught up into paradise and heard things that cannot be told, which man may not utter. Paul cannot and will not reveal what he heard. His silence stands in sharp contrast to his opponents' eagerness to publicize their experiences.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Paradise', 'explanation': "From the Persian word for 'enclosed garden.' In Jewish thought, paradise was the heavenly realm of the blessed dead, associated with Eden restored. Jesus promised the thief on the cross: 'Today you will be with me in paradise' (Luke 23:43)."}

verses.text: On behalf of such a one I will boast, but on my own behalf I will not boast, except in my weaknesses.
verses.explainer: On behalf of this man Paul will boast, but on his own behalf he will not boast, except of his weaknesses. The split is deliberate: the heavenly experience belongs to the 'man in Christ'; Paul's own identity is defined by weakness.

verses.text: For if I would desire to boast, I will not be foolish; for I will speak the truth. But I refrain, so that no man may think more of me than that which he sees in me, or hears from me.
verses.explainer: Even if Paul wished to boast, he would not be a fool because he would be speaking the truth. But he refrains so that no one may think more of him than what they see and hear from him. Paul wants to be evaluated by his observable life, not his private experiences.

verses.text: By reason of the exceeding greatness of the revelations, that I should not be exalted excessively, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satan to torment me, that I should not be exalted excessively.
verses.explainer: To keep Paul from becoming conceited because of the surpassing greatness of the revelations, a thorn was given him in the flesh, a messenger of Satan to harass him. The thorn serves a dual function: it is a satanic attack and a divine safeguard against pride.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's opponents would have seen chronic affliction as evidence of divine disfavor. A true apostle blessed by God would not suffer from an unhealed condition. Paul completely inverts this logic.
thenVsNow.now: The 'thorn in the flesh' has become one of the most discussed concepts in Christian experience. It validates the reality that God sometimes answers prayer with 'no' and that unanswered prayer does not indicate lack of faith.

verses.text: Concerning this thing, I begged the Lord three times that it might depart from me.
verses.explainer: Three times Paul pleaded with the Lord to remove the thorn. The number three echoes Jesus' three prayers in Gethsemane to remove the cup of suffering. Like Jesus, Paul receives a 'no' -- but with a profound reason.

verses.text:
He has said to me,
“My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.” Most gladly therefore I will rather glory in my weaknesses, that the power of Christ may rest on me.
verses.explainer: The Lord said to Paul: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.' This is the most important verse in 2 Corinthians and arguably one of the most important in all of Paul's letters. God's power and human weakness are not opposites -- they are partners. God's strength is most fully displayed through human fragility.

verses.text: Therefore I take pleasure in weaknesses, in injuries, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses, for Christ’s sake. For when I am weak, then am I strong.
verses.explainer: For the sake of Christ, Paul is content with weaknesses, insults, hardships, persecutions, and calamities. For when he is weak, then he is strong. This is the ultimate paradox and the key that unlocks the entire letter. Paul's weakness is not a liability to be overcome but a strategy to be embraced.

thenVsNow.then: The Roman world worshiped power, strength, and victory. Paul's celebration of weakness was genuinely countercultural -- it sounded like madness to Greco-Roman ears.
thenVsNow.now: This verse continues to challenge every culture that equates strength with value. For anyone experiencing chronic illness, disability, failure, or limitation, Paul offers a radical reframing: your weakness is the place where God's power shows up most clearly.

verses.text: I have become foolish in boasting. You compelled me, for I ought to have been commended by you, for in nothing was I inferior to the very best apostles, though I am nothing.
verses.explainer: Paul has been a fool. The Corinthians drove him to it. He ought to have been commended by them, for he is not at all inferior to the super-apostles, even though he is 'nothing.' The fool's speech is over. Paul blames the Corinthians for making it necessary.

verses.text: Truly the signs of an apostle were worked among you in all perseverance, in signs and wonders and mighty works.
verses.explainer: The signs of a true apostle were performed among the Corinthians with utmost patience -- signs, wonders, and mighty works. Paul did perform miracles in Corinth. His opponents cannot claim otherwise.

verses.text: For what is there in which you were made inferior to the rest of the assemblies, unless it is that I myself was not a burden to you? Forgive me this wrong.
verses.explainer: In what way were the Corinthians treated worse than other churches, except that Paul himself did not burden them financially? He sarcastically asks them to forgive him for this 'wrong.' His refusal of payment is ironically presented as an offense.

verses.text: Behold, this is the third time I am ready to come to you, and I will not be a burden to you; for I seek not your possessions, but you. For the children ought not to save up for the parents, but the parents for the children.
verses.explainer: Paul is ready to come to Corinth for the third time, and he will not be a burden. He seeks not their possessions but them. Parents save up for their children, not children for their parents.

verses.text: I will most gladly spend and be spent for your souls. If I love you more abundantly, am I loved the less?
verses.explainer: Paul will most gladly spend and be spent for their souls. If he loves them more, is he to be loved less? The emotional intensity is raw. Paul's love for the Corinthians is costing him everything, and they are returning less and less.

verses.text: But be it so, I did not myself burden you. But, being crafty, I caught you with deception.
verses.explainer: Paul did not burden them. But some say he was crafty and took them in by deceit. Even his financial integrity is twisted into an accusation -- perhaps he is laundering money through the collection!

verses.text: Did I take advantage of you by anyone of them whom I have sent to you?
verses.explainer: Did Paul take advantage of the Corinthians through anyone he sent? He challenges them to name a single instance of financial exploitation.

verses.text: I exhorted Titus, and I sent the brother with him. Did Titus take any advantage of you? Didn’t we walk in the same spirit? Didn’t we walk in the same steps?
verses.explainer: Paul urged Titus to visit and sent the brother with him. Did Titus take advantage of them? They walked in the same spirit and the same steps. Paul's associates are equally above reproach.

verses.text: Again, do you think that we are excusing ourselves to you? In the sight of God we speak in Christ. But all things, beloved, are for your edifying.
verses.explainer: Paul has been defending himself, but everything he does is for building up the Corinthians. Even his self-defense serves their edification, not his ego.

verses.text: For I am afraid that by any means, when I come, I might find you not the way I want to, and that I might be found by you as you don’t desire; that by any means there would be strife, jealousy, outbursts of anger, factions, slander, whisperings, proud thoughts, riots;
verses.explainer: Paul fears that when he comes he may find quarreling, jealousy, anger, selfishness, slander, gossip, conceit, and disorder. This list of sins suggests the Corinthian problems described in 1 Corinthians have not been fully resolved.

verses.text: that again when I come my God would humble me before you, and I would mourn for many of those who have sinned before now, and not repented of the uncleanness and sexual immorality and lustfulness which they committed.
verses.explainer: Paul is afraid that God will humble him before the Corinthians, and he will mourn over many who have sinned previously and have not repented of impurity, sexual immorality, and sensuality. The sexual ethics problems also persist. Paul approaches his third visit with dread, not confidence.

explainer.summary: Paul issues final warnings before his third visit. He will not be lenient -- every charge will be established by two or three witnesses. He calls the Corinthians to examine themselves to see whether they are in the faith. The letter closes with one of the most beautiful benedictions in the Bible: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.'
explainer.contextInBook: This brief chapter brings the letter full circle. Paul's third visit is imminent, and he wants the Corinthians to prepare by self-examination rather than face his discipline. The trinitarian benediction at the end has become the most widely used blessing in Christian worship.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Paul uses two different words for testing: peirazo
wordStudies.meaning: Paul uses two different words for testing: peirazo (to test, put on trial) and dokimazo (to prove, approve after testing). He asks the Corinthians to examine whether their faith is genuine, applying the same scrutiny to themselves that they apply to him.

verses.text: This is the third time I am coming to you. “At the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established.”
verses.explainer: This is the third time Paul is coming to them. Every charge must be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses. He invokes the Deuteronomic legal standard for his coming visit.

verses.text: I have said beforehand, and I do say beforehand, as when I was present the second time, so now, being absent, I write to those who have sinned before now, and to all the rest, that, if I come again, I will not spare;
verses.explainer: Paul warned those who sinned before and all the others, and he warns them now while absent: if he comes again, he will not spare them. This is not an empty threat. Paul's gentleness should not be mistaken for tolerance of unrepentant sin.

verses.text: seeing that you seek a proof of Christ who speaks in me; who toward you is not weak, but is powerful in you.
verses.explainer: The Corinthians want proof that Christ speaks in Paul. Christ is not weak in dealing with them but is powerful among them. They questioned Paul's authority; Paul says testing him means testing Christ.

verses.text: For he was crucified through weakness, yet he lives through the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but we will live with him through the power of God toward you.
verses.explainer: Christ was crucified in weakness but lives by the power of God. Paul is weak in Christ but will live with Christ by the power of God directed toward the Corinthians. The pattern of the entire letter compressed into one verse: weakness followed by divine power. Christ's cross looked like defeat; the resurrection proved it was victory.

thenVsNow.then: The crucifixion was the most shameful form of execution in the Roman world. Claiming that the crucified Jesus was Lord was an offense to every Roman value of power and honor.
thenVsNow.now: The 'weakness-to-power' pattern remains the distinctive shape of Christian life and ministry. It challenges every model of leadership built on dominance rather than service.

verses.text: Examine your own selves, whether you are in the faith. Test your own selves. Or don’t you know as to your own selves, that Jesus Christ is in you?—unless indeed you are disqualified.
verses.explainer: Examine yourselves to see whether you are in the faith. Test yourselves. Do you not realize that Jesus Christ is in you? -- unless indeed you fail to meet the test! Paul redirects the testing. Instead of testing him, the Corinthians should test themselves.

verses.text: But I hope that you will know that we aren’t disqualified.
verses.explainer: Paul hopes they will find that he has not failed the test. If their faith is genuine (Christ is in them), then Paul -- who brought them the gospel -- has not failed either.

verses.text: Now I pray to God that you do no evil; not that we may appear approved, but that you may do that which is honorable, though we are as reprobate.
verses.explainer: Paul prays that the Corinthians will do no wrong -- not so that Paul may appear to have passed the test, but so that they may do what is right, even if Paul seems to have failed. He cares more about their holiness than his reputation.

verses.text: For we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth.
verses.explainer: Paul cannot do anything against the truth, only for the truth. His authority is limited to building up, not tearing down. Even apostolic power has boundaries set by truth.

verses.text: For we rejoice when we are weak and you are strong. And this we also pray for, even your perfecting.
verses.explainer: Paul is glad when he is weak and the Corinthians are strong. He prays for their restoration. The word 'restoration' (katartisis) means being put in proper order, like setting a broken bone. Paul wants them whole.

verses.text: For this cause I write these things while absent, that I may not deal sharply when present, according to the authority which the Lord gave me for building up, and not for tearing down.
verses.explainer: Paul writes these things while absent so that when he comes he may not have to be severe in his use of authority, which the Lord gave for building up and not for tearing down. The purpose of the letter is to prevent the need for harsh discipline on his arrival.

verses.text: Finally, brothers, rejoice. Be perfected, be comforted, be of the same mind, live in peace, and the God of love and peace will be with you.
verses.explainer: Finally, brothers, rejoice. Aim for restoration, comfort one another, agree with one another, live in peace, and the God of love and peace will be with you. Five imperatives compressed into one sentence -- a blueprint for community health.

verses.text: Greet one another with a holy kiss.
verses.explainer: Greet one another with a holy kiss. This was a standard greeting in the early church, signifying affection and unity among believers.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Holy kiss', 'explanation': 'A kiss on the cheek was a common greeting in the ancient Mediterranean world. The early church adopted it as a liturgical practice, signifying the peace and unity of believers. Paul mentions it in multiple letters (Romans 16:16, 1 Corinthians 16:20, 1 Thessalonians 5:26).'}

verses.text: All the saints greet you.
verses.explainer: All the saints greet the Corinthians. Paul extends greetings from the broader Christian community, reminding Corinth that they are part of something larger than their local conflicts.

verses.text: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, God’s love, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit, be with you all. Amen.
verses.explainer: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all. This trinitarian benediction is the most explicitly triadic formula in Paul's letters and has become the most widely used blessing in Christian worship across traditions.

thenVsNow.then: Paul did not set out to articulate a doctrine of the Trinity, but this benediction attributes distinct roles to three divine persons: grace from Christ, love from God the Father, and fellowship from the Holy Spirit.
thenVsNow.now: This benediction is spoken in virtually every Christian worship service worldwide. It is the closing blessing in Catholic, Orthodox, Anglican, Lutheran, Reformed, and many evangelical traditions -- a rare point of unity across a deeply divided church.
